canary.method: openpyxl writes formulas with NO cached <v> value; `soffice --headless --convert-to xlsx` must evaluate every formula from scratch to produce any output value at all. The volatile =NOW() canary changing between two runs a few seconds apart, plus the deterministic arithmetic canary matching exactly, together prove genuine recalculation.

ABS_zero.description: Zero is its own absolute value

ABS_expression_argument.description: Argument can be any expression evaluating to a number

ABS_cell_reference_negative.description: Absolute value of a negative cell reference

ABS_error_propagates.description: Errors in the argument propagate through ABS unchanged rather than being suppressed

AND_range_numeric_with_zero.notes: Numeric coercion in ranges confirmed via https://www.ablebits.com/office-addins-blog/excel-and-or-xor-not-functions/ since Microsoft's own AND page doesn't give an explicit numeric example.

AND_range_numeric_all_nonzero.description: A range of all-nonzero numbers coerces to all-TRUE -> TRUE

ARRAYTOTEXT_default_format.description: Default (concise) format joins elements with comma+space

ARRAYTOTEXT_strict_format.description: format=1 (strict) reproduces array-literal syntax with braces

AVERAGE_ignores_text_and_blanks.description: Text values and blank cells in the range are excluded from both the sum and the count, rather than being treated as 0
AVERAGE_ignores_text_and_blanks.notes: Only A1, A3, A5 (10, 20, 30) are numeric; average = 60/3 = 20. If blanks/text counted as 0 the result would be 12

AVERAGE_all_text_div0.description: A range containing only text has zero numeric values to average -> #DIV/0!

AVERAGE_empty_range_div0.description: A fully empty range also has zero numeric values -> #DIV/0!

AVERAGEIF_separate_average_range.description: Criteria evaluated against A but matching B cells are averaged
AVERAGEIF_separate_average_range.notes: Rows 3,4,5 satisfy A>2; average of paired B = (30+40+50)/3

AVERAGEIF_no_match_div0.description: No cells satisfy the criteria -> #DIV/0! per Microsoft docs (same as AVERAGE of an empty set)

AVERAGEIF_blank_average_range_cells_excl.description: Blank cells in average_range are excluded from both the sum and the count, not treated as 0
AVERAGEIF_blank_average_range_cells_excl.notes: A1:A3 all satisfy >0, but B2 is blank; average is (10+30)/2=20, not (10+0+30)/3

AVERAGEIFS_basic.description: Average of A where paired B > 2

AVERAGEIFS_multi_criteria.notes: Only rows 2,3 satisfy B>1 AND C<5; average of 20,30 = 25

AVERAGEIFS_wildcard.notes: apple and avocado match a*; average of 10,30 = 20

CEILING.MATH_basic_significance.description: Rounds 24.3 up to the nearest multiple of 5. Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/ceiling-math-function-80f95d2f-b499-4eee-9f16-f795a8e306c8 (worked example table)

CEILING.MATH_default_significance_1.description: Significance and mode both omitted -> defaults to nearest integer (significance 1). Source: Microsoft CEILING.MATH docs.

CEILING.MATH_negative_default_mode_towar.description: Negative number, mode omitted -> unlike the modeless legacy CEILING function, CEILING.MATH's default for negative numbers rounds TOWARD zero.
CEILING.MATH_negative_default_mode_towar.notes: Microsoft worked example: CEILING.MATH(-8.1,2) = -8 ('rounds -8.1 up (toward 0) to the nearest multiple of 2'). This is the modern, explicit-mode successor to legacy CEILING and was designed to match LibreOffice/ODF's CEILING(...,...,Mode) semantics for the default case.

CEILING.MATH_negative_mode_nonzero_away_.description: Negative number with a nonzero mode argument -> rounds away from zero instead of the toward-zero default.
CEILING.MATH_negative_mode_nonzero_away_.notes: Microsoft worked example: CEILING.MATH(-5.5,2,-1) = -6 ('rounds -5.5 down (away from 0) ... with a mode of -1'). Mode only affects negative numbers; any nonzero mode reverses the default direction.

CEILING_basic.description: Basic case, rounds 2.5 up to the nearest multiple of 1. Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/ceiling-function-0a5cd7c8-0720-4f0a-bd2c-c943e510899f (worked example table)

CEILING_fractional_significance.description: Rounds 0.234 up to the nearest multiple of 0.01. Source: Microsoft CEILING docs worked example table.

CEILING_both_negative_away_from_zero.description: Number and significance both negative -> rounds away from zero (more negative), per Microsoft's documented rule and worked example.
CEILING_both_negative_away_from_zero.notes: Microsoft: 'If number is negative, and significance is negative, the value is rounded down, away from zero.' Worked example CEILING(-2.5,-2)=-4 at https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/ceiling-function-0a5cd7c8-0720-4f0a-bd2c-c943e510899f

CEILING_negative_number_positive_signifi.description: Number negative, significance positive -> rounds up toward zero (less negative), per Microsoft's documented rule and worked example.
CEILING_negative_number_positive_signifi.notes: Microsoft: 'If number is negative, and significance is positive, the value is rounded up towards zero.' Worked example CEILING(-2.5,2)=-2 at the same doc page.

CEILING_libreoffice_default_mode_diverge.description: GOLD QUIRK CANDIDATE: identical 2-argument call (Mode omitted) with number and significance both negative. Per Microsoft's documented CEILING rule this must round AWAY from zero.
CEILING_libreoffice_default_mode_diverge.notes: Microsoft (https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/ceiling-function-0a5cd7c8-0720-4f0a-bd2c-c943e510899f): both-negative case rounds away from zero, so CEILING(-45.67,-2) -> -46 (ratio -45.67/-2=22.835, ceiling(22.835)=23, 23*-2=-46). LibreOffice's own CEILING docs (https://help.libreoffice.org/latest/en-US/text/scalc/01/func_ceiling.html) define a 3rd optional Mode argument that ONLY applies when both Number and Significance are negative, and state: 'if Mode is equal to zero or not given, negative numbers are rounded up (towards zero)' -- with LO's own worked example CEILING(-45.67;-2;0) = -44. That means the identical 2-arg formula (Mode omitted) is documented to resolve to -44 under LibreOffice's own default, vs -46 under Microsoft's documented default -- a genuine cross-engine default-behavior divergence for the exact same formula text, not just a wording difference. If LO's executed result is -44, matched_expected:false here is the intended, correct signal of that divergence -- do not 'fix' this expectation to match LO.

CEILING_nonnumeric_error.description: Nonnumeric first argument -> #VALUE!, per Microsoft docs: 'If either argument is nonnumeric, CEILING returns the #VALUE! error value.'

CHAR_out_of_range_zero.description: 0 is outside CHAR's documented valid range of 1-255 -> #VALUE!
CHAR_out_of_range_zero.notes: Microsoft docs: CHAR takes "A number between 1 and 255 specifying which character you want." Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/char-function-bbd249c8-b36e-4a91-8017-1c133f9b837a; MISMATCH vs expected: expected '#VALUE!', got '_x0000_'

CHAR_out_of_range_256.description: 256 is above CHAR's documented valid range of 1-255 -> #VALUE!
CHAR_out_of_range_256.notes: Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/char-function-bbd249c8-b36e-4a91-8017-1c133f9b837a - valid range is 1-255; values outside it are invalid

CHOOSE_basic_second_value.description: index_num=2 selects the second value in the list

CHOOSE_out_of_range_error.description: index_num beyond the number of supplied values -> #VALUE!
CHOOSE_out_of_range_error.notes: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/choose-function-fc5c184f-cb62-4ec7-a46e-38653b98f5bc -- 'If index_num is less than 1 or greater than the number of the last value in the list, CHOOSE returns the #VALUE! error value.'

CHOOSE_fractional_index_truncated.description: Non-integer index_num is truncated toward the lowest integer before use, not rounded
CHOOSE_fractional_index_truncated.notes: Same doc as above: 'If index_num is a fraction, it is truncated to the lowest integer before being used.' 2.9 truncates to 2, not rounded to 3

CHOOSE_selects_a_range_then_summed.description: CHOOSE can select an entire range as its 'value', which is then fed to SUM
CHOOSE_selects_a_range_then_summed.notes: index_num=2 selects the B1:B3 range; SUM of 10+20+30=60

CHOOSECOLS_pick_3_1.description: Return columns 3 and 1 of a row, in that order
CHOOSECOLS_pick_3_1.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 3, got '#NAME?'

CHOOSEROWS_pick_4_2.description: Return rows 4 and 2 of a column, in that order
CHOOSEROWS_pick_4_2.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 40, got '#NAME?'

CLEAN_removes_control_char.description: CHAR(7) (BEL, a non-printable 7-bit ASCII control character) is stripped out

CLEAN_removes_tab_leaves_text.description: CLEAN removes CHAR(9) (tab) because it's one of the first 32 non-printable ASCII codes -- contrast with TRIM, which leaves tabs completely untouched since TRIM only targets the space character (32), not control characters
CLEAN_removes_tab_leaves_text.notes: Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/clean-function-26f3d7c5-475f-4a9c-90e5-4b8ba987ba41 - CLEAN "removes nonprintable characters from ASCII codes 0 through 31"; its own documented example uses CHAR(9)&"Monthly report"&CHAR(10) -> "Monthly report"

CLEAN_leaves_spaces_alone.description: Regular space characters (code 32) are left alone by CLEAN -- only non-printable control characters (codes 0-31) are removed

CLEAN_no_op_on_clean_text.description: Text with no non-printable characters passes through unchanged

CODE_first_character_only.description: CODE only looks at the first character of a multi-character string
CODE_first_character_only.notes: Microsoft docs: CODE "Returns a numeric code for the first character in a text string." Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/code-function-c32b692b-2ed0-4a04-bdd9-75640144b928

CODE_char_roundtrip.description: CODE and CHAR are exact inverses of each other for values 1-255

COLUMN_no_arg_uses_formula_cell.description: No argument: COLUMN() returns the column of the cell containing the formula itself. This case's formula lands in F1 by default, and F is the 6th column
COLUMN_no_arg_uses_formula_cell.notes: F is column 6; COLUMN() with no anchor argument returns the formula's own column

COLUMN_explicit_cell_ref.description: Explicit single-cell reference argument overrides the formula's own location

COLUMN_range_returns_first_column.description: In non-array (scalar) context, COLUMN() of a multi-column range returns the column number of the FIRST column of the reference

COLUMN_far_column.description: Sanity check with a larger column number (Z is the 26th column)

COLUMNS_basic_row_range.description: Width (column count) of a single-row range; does not depend on cell contents

COLUMNS_single_cell.description: A single cell reference has exactly 1 column

COLUMNS_2d_range.description: Column count of a 2-D range only counts columns, ignoring the 4 rows

CONCAT_range.description: CONCAT flattens a range directly (unlike CONCATENATE, which cannot take a range)

CONCAT_mixed_types.description: Numbers are coerced to text without an explicit TEXT() call

CONCATENATE_cell_refs.description: Join cells with a space separator

COUNT_mixed_types_range.description: COUNT only counts numeric cells in a range; text, blanks, and booleans (when referenced via a range) are all excluded
COUNT_mixed_types_range.notes: A3 and A6 are blank, A2 is text, A4 is a boolean cell; only A1=10 and A5=20 are counted; MISMATCH vs expected: expected 2, got 3

COUNT_boolean_in_range_excluded.description: A boolean value referenced through a cell range is NOT counted by COUNT
COUNT_boolean_in_range_excluded.notes: Per Microsoft's COUNT documentation, booleans in a referenced range are excluded from COUNT, unlike booleans typed directly as literal arguments; MISMATCH vs expected: expected 0, got 1

COUNT_boolean_literal_argument_counted.description: Contrast with COUNT_boolean_in_range_excluded: a logical value typed directly into the argument list IS counted
COUNT_boolean_literal_argument_counted.notes: Microsoft's COUNT docs: "Logical values and text representations of numbers that you type directly into the list of arguments are counted" - this differs from the same boolean sitting in a cell reference

COUNT_all_blank_range.description: A range with no values at all counts as 0

COUNTA_mixed_types_range.description: COUNTA counts every non-blank cell regardless of type: numbers, text, and booleans all count, unlike COUNT which only counts numbers (same setup as COUNT_mixed_types_range)
COUNTA_mixed_types_range.notes: A1, A2, A4, A5 are all non-blank (A3, A6 are genuinely blank and excluded)

COUNTA_empty_string_formula_counts.description: A cell holding a formula that returns "" is still counted by COUNTA, because it contains content (a formula), even though it displays as empty. Confirmed via Microsoft's COUNTA docs (https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/counta-function-7dc98875-d5c1-46f1-9a82-53f3219e2509): "if the range contains a formula that returns an empty string, the COUNTA function counts that value."
COUNTA_empty_string_formula_counts.notes: Both A1 (formula returning "") and A2 (5) are counted; this same A1 cell is ALSO counted as blank by COUNTBLANK - see COUNTBLANK_empty_string_formula_counted_as_blank

COUNTA_all_blank_range.description: A range with no content at all counts as 0

COUNTBLANK_basic.description: Counts genuinely empty cells in a range
COUNTBLANK_basic.notes: A2 and A4 are the blank cells

COUNTBLANK_mixed_types_range.description: Same mixed range as COUNT/COUNTA: only the genuinely empty cells count, not the text/number/boolean cells

COUNTBLANK_empty_string_formula_counted_.description: Documented cross-engine-relevant edge case: a cell containing a formula that returns "" is counted as blank by COUNTBLANK, same as a truly empty cell - even though that identical cell would be counted as non-blank by COUNTA (see COUNTA_empty_string_formula_counts). Confirmed via Microsoft's COUNTBLANK docs (https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/countblank-function-6a92d772-675c-4bee-b346-24af6bd3ac22): "Cells with formulas that return "" (empty text) are also counted."
COUNTBLANK_empty_string_formula_counted_.notes: A1 is truly blank (not set at all) and A2's formula result of "" is ALSO counted as blank -> 2 total; only A3=5 is excluded

COUNTBLANK_zero_not_blank.description: A cell containing the number 0 is NOT counted as blank

COUNTIF_case_insensitive_text_match.description: Text comparisons in COUNTIF are case-insensitive
COUNTIF_case_insensitive_text_match.notes: Both 'apple' and 'Apple' match the literal 'APPLE' criteria regardless of case

COUNTIF_criteria_from_cell_concat.description: Dynamic criteria built by concatenating an operator with a cell reference

COUNTIFS_multi_range.notes: Rows 2,3 satisfy A>10 AND B<5 (rows 4,5 fail B<5); rows matching: (20,2),(30,3)

DATE_month_rollover_above_12.description: Documented rollover: month=13 adds 13 months worth of offset from January, landing in January of the FOLLOWING year rather than raising an error. Verified via https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/date-function-e36c0c8c-4104-49da-ab83-82328b832349 ("If month is greater than 12 ... DATE(2008,14,2) returns ... February 2, 2009")
DATE_month_rollover_above_12.notes: month 13 = 12 months past January 2024 -> January 2025; 2025-01-01 = serial 45658 (2024 is a leap year, 366 days after 45292)

DATE_month_rollback_below_1.description: Documented rollback: month<=0 subtracts months backward across a year boundary. Verified via the same DATE documentation ("If month is less than 1 ... DATE(2008,-3,2) returns ... September 2, 2007")

DATEDIF_md_known_quirk.description: "MD" (days ignoring months and years) is documented by Microsoft as unreliable/buggy for some date combos
DATEDIF_md_known_quirk.notes: Textbook-naive expectation might be ~1, but MD has a well-known Microsoft-acknowledged bug around month-end dates; recording the ACTUAL engine output is the point of this test

DATEDIF_end_before_start.description: End date before start date -> #NUM! per Microsoft's documented DATEDIF behavior
DATEDIF_end_before_start.notes: Microsoft docs state end<start raises #NUM!; record engines' ACTUAL error code here since this is a known point of cross-engine divergence; MISMATCH vs expected: expected '#NUM!', got '#VALUE!'

DAY_of_date.description: DAY applied to a constructed date

DAY_of_raw_serial_epoch.description: DAY applied directly to the raw serial number 1 (Jan 1, 1900)
DAY_of_raw_serial_epoch.notes: Serial 1 is Jan 1, 1900 in the 1900 date system; MISMATCH vs expected: expected 1, got 31

DAY_ignores_time_fraction.description: A serial with a fractional (time-of-day) component still resolves to the correct calendar day
DAY_ignores_time_fraction.notes: 45366 = 2024-03-15; the .75 fractional day is ignored by DAY

DAYS_basic_end_first_argument_order.description: DAYS takes END_DATE FIRST, then start_date -- the opposite argument order from DAYS360/YEARFRAC. Verified via https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/days-function-57740535-d549-4395-8728-0f07bff0b9df ("DAYS(end_date, start_date)")

DAYS_reversed_arguments_flip_sign.description: Swapping which date is passed first (now the earlier date is 'end_date') flips the sign -- reinforcing that argument order is significant and not auto-corrected
DAYS_reversed_arguments_flip_sign.notes: Same two dates as DAYS_basic_end_first_argument_order but with roles swapped -> -14

DAYS_doc_example_text_dates.description: Microsoft's own documented example, using text dates parsed as if by DATEVALUE
DAYS_doc_example_text_dates.notes: Exact example from the DAYS function documentation

DAYS_doc_example_full_year.description: A second literal example from the same Microsoft documentation page

DAYS360_doc_example_basic.description: Microsoft's own documented example, default (US/NASD) method. Verified via https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/days360-function-b9a509fd-49ef-407e-94df-0cbda5718c2a

DAYS360_doc_example_full_year.description: A second literal example from the same documentation page, showing the 30-day-month/360-day-year convention

DAYS360_us_method_month_end_shift.description: US (NASD) method month-end rule: end_date is the last day of its month (Jan 31) and start_date (Jan 1) is before the 30th, so end_date shifts to the 1st of the NEXT month (Feb 1) before the 30/360 day count. Verified via the DAYS360 documentation's description of the US method's end-of-month handling

DAYS360_european_method_month_end_no_shi.description: Same date pair as DAYS360_us_method_month_end_shift but method=TRUE (European method): the European rule simply clamps day-31 dates to day 30, with none of the US method's 'shift end_date to next month' logic, giving a genuinely DIFFERENT result (29, not 30) for the identical inputs -- the documented US-vs-European divergence
DAYS360_european_method_month_end_no_shi.notes: Effective dates become Jan 1 -> Jan 30 (31 clamped to 30, no next-month shift) -> (1-1)*30 + (30-1) = 29

DEGREES_pi_is_180.description: Pi radians is exactly a half turn, i.e. 180 degrees

DEGREES_negative.description: Negative radians convert to negative degrees (a quarter turn backward)

DROP_first_row_and_col.description: Drop the first row and first column of a 3x3 range
DROP_first_row_and_col.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 5, got '#NAME?'

EDATE_leap_year_clamp.description: Jan 31 + 1 month clamps to the last day of February in a leap year (2024-02-29); result is the underlying date serial number
EDATE_leap_year_clamp.notes: 2024 is a leap year; Feb has no 31st so EDATE clamps to Feb 29 = serial 45351

EDATE_negative_months.description: Negative months argument goes backward (2024-01-15 = serial 45306)

EDATE_fractional_months_truncated.description: Non-integer months argument is truncated toward zero, not rounded (2024-02-01 = serial 45323)

EOMONTH_zero_months_same_month.description: months=0 returns the end of the SAME month as the given date

EOMONTH_positive_months_forward.description: Positive months argument moves forward to the end of a future month

EOMONTH_negative_months_backward.description: Negative months argument moves backward to the end of a past month
EOMONTH_negative_months_backward.notes: End of February 2024 (a leap year) = 2024-02-29 = serial 45351

EOMONTH_crosses_year_boundary.description: Positive offset that also crosses a year boundary

ERROR.TYPE_null.description: Space-intersection of two non-overlapping ranges produces a genuine #NULL! error -> ERROR.TYPE code 1

ERROR.TYPE_ref.description: Offsetting above row 1 produces a genuine #REF! error -> code 4

ERROR.TYPE_name.description: An undefined name reference produces #NAME? -> code 5

ERROR.TYPE_num.description: Square root of a negative number produces #NUM! -> code 6

ERROR.TYPE_non_error.description: Per docs, applying ERROR.TYPE to anything that is NOT an error value itself returns the #N/A error
ERROR.TYPE_non_error.notes: Confirmed via https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/error-type-function-10958677-7c8d-44f7-ae77-b9a9ee6eefaa: 'returns the #N/A error' for non-error input.

EXACT_case_sensitive_false.description: EXACT is case-sensitive: differing case alone makes two otherwise-identical strings unequal

EXACT_space_sensitive.description: EXACT is sensitive to spacing differences, matching Microsoft's own documented example
EXACT_space_sensitive.notes: Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/exact-function-d3087698-fc15-4a15-9631-12575cf29926 - documented example compares 'w ord' vs 'word' -> FALSE

EXACT_numeric_arguments.description: EXACT also accepts numeric values directly; identical numbers compare equal

EXP_zero.description: e raised to the 0th power is 1

EXP_one_is_e.description: EXP(1) returns Euler's number e itself

EXP_ln_roundtrip.description: EXP is documented as the inverse of LN; composing them round-trips the original value

FALSE_zero_arg_function.description: The zero-argument function form FALSE() is callable and returns the logical value FALSE, per https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/false-function-2d58dfa5-9c03-4259-bf8f-f0ae14346904

FALSE_equals_keyword.description: FALSE() returns a genuine logical value equal to the bare FALSE keyword

FALSE_in_if.description: FALSE() used as a condition behaves exactly like the FALSE keyword

FILTER_basic.description: Spill the subset of A1:A5 where the paired B value is >2
FILTER_basic.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 'c', got '#NAME?'

FILTER_all_false_with_default.description: if_empty argument is returned when no rows match

FILTER_all_false_no_default.description: No if_empty and no matches raises #CALC!

FILTER_2d_by_column_condition.description: Filtering a 2-column range by a condition on the first column
FILTER_2d_by_column_condition.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 2, got '#NAME?'

FILTER_inline_array_literal.description: Filtering an inline array literal by an inline boolean/number mask
FILTER_inline_array_literal.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 1, got '#NAME?'

FIND_basic.description: Basic case-sensitive positional search for the first occurrence

FIND_case_sensitive_no_match.description: FIND is case-sensitive: lowercase 'a' does not match the uppercase 'A' in "Apple", and there is no other 'a' -> #VALUE! (contrast with SEARCH, which is case-insensitive and would find it)
FIND_case_sensitive_no_match.notes: Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-gb/office/find-function-06213f91-b5be-4544-8b0b-2fd5a775436f and https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/how-to-correct-a-value-error-in-find-findb-and-search-searchb-functions-0d1299aa-6234-4253-bc5e-50881b575fc6 - FIND is case-sensitive and does not support wildcard characters

FIND_not_found.description: find_text not present anywhere in within_text -> #VALUE!

FIND_start_num_skips_earlier_match.description: start_num=3 begins the search at position 3, skipping the earlier match of 'p' at position 2

FLOOR.MATH_basic_significance.description: Rounds 24.3 down to the nearest multiple of 5. Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/floor-math-function-c302b599-fbdb-4177-ba19-2c2b1249a2f5 (worked example table)

FLOOR.MATH_default_significance_1.description: Significance and mode both omitted -> defaults to nearest integer (significance 1). Source: Microsoft FLOOR.MATH docs.

FLOOR.MATH_negative_default_mode_away_fr.description: Negative number, mode omitted -> FLOOR.MATH's default for negative numbers rounds AWAY from zero (opposite default direction from CEILING.MATH's toward-zero default).
FLOOR.MATH_negative_default_mode_away_fr.notes: Microsoft worked example: FLOOR.MATH(-8.1,2) = -10 ('rounds -8.1 down (away from 0) to the nearest integer that is a multiple of 2'). Note this default is the mirror image of CEILING.MATH's default (toward zero) for the same-shaped call.

FLOOR.MATH_negative_mode_nonzero_toward_.description: Negative number with a nonzero mode argument -> reverses the default to round toward zero instead of away from zero.
FLOOR.MATH_negative_mode_nonzero_toward_.notes: Microsoft worked example: FLOOR.MATH(-5.5,2,-1) = -4 ('rounds -5.5 toward 0 ... using a non-zero Mode, which reverses rounding direction').

FLOOR_basic.description: Basic case, rounds 3.7 down to the nearest multiple of 2. Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/floor-function-14bb497c-24f2-4e04-b327-b0b4de5a8886 (worked example table)

FLOOR_fractional_significance.description: Rounds 0.234 down to the nearest multiple of 0.01. Source: Microsoft FLOOR docs worked example table.

FLOOR_both_negative_toward_zero.description: Number and significance both negative -> Microsoft's own worked example rounds this TOWARD zero (-2), which is the opposite direction CEILING takes for the same-signed case.
FLOOR_both_negative_toward_zero.notes: Microsoft's worked example table gives FLOOR(-2.5,-2) = -2 exactly (https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/floor-function-14bb497c-24f2-4e04-b327-b0b4de5a8886). Note this is the OPPOSITE rounding direction of CEILING(-2.5,-2)=-4 for the identical arguments -- FLOOR and CEILING deliberately diverge on both-negative inputs, not a bug.

FLOOR_mismatched_sign_error.description: Number positive, significance negative (opposite signs) -> #NUM!, explicitly documented by Microsoft: 'If Number is positive and Significance is negative, FLOOR returns the #NUM! error value.'

FLOOR_libreoffice_default_mode_divergenc.description: GOLD QUIRK CANDIDATE: mirror image of the CEILING default-Mode divergence. Both Number and significance negative, Mode omitted.
FLOOR_libreoffice_default_mode_divergenc.notes: Derived from Microsoft's own worked FLOOR(-2.5,-2)=-2 example (both-negative case rounds TOWARD zero): ratio -45.67/-2=22.835, truncating toward zero gives 22, 22*-2=-44. LibreOffice's own FLOOR docs (https://help.libreoffice.org/latest/en-US/text/scalc/01/func_floor.html) define an optional 3rd Mode argument that only applies when both Number and Significance are negative, stating 'Mode = 0 or omitted: negative numbers are rounded down (away from zero)' with LO's own worked example FLOOR(-45.67;-2;0) = -46. So the identical 2-arg formula (Mode omitted) is documented to resolve to -46 under LibreOffice's default vs -44 under Microsoft's documented behavior -- a genuine divergence, and the exact mirror image of the CEILING case above (there Excel defaults away-from-zero and LO defaults toward-zero; here Excel defaults toward-zero and LO defaults away-from-zero). If LO's executed result is -46, matched_expected:false is the correct, intended signal -- do not 'fix' this expectation to match LO.

FLOOR_nonnumeric_error.description: Nonnumeric first argument -> #VALUE!, per Microsoft docs: 'If either argument is nonnumeric, FLOOR returns the #VALUE! error value.'

FORMULATEXT_basic_arithmetic.description: A cell holding a plain arithmetic formula -> FORMULATEXT returns the exact formula text as typed

FORMULATEXT_preserves_exact_text.description: Formula text with nested string literals is returned verbatim, including quotes

FORMULATEXT_non_formula_cell.description: A cell containing a plain (non-formula) value -> #N/A, per docs: 'The cell used as the Reference argument does not contain a formula.' Confirmed via https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/formulatext-function-0a786771-54fd-4ae2-96ee-09cda35439c8

FORMULATEXT_blank_cell.description: A blank cell has no formula either -> #N/A

HLOOKUP_exact_match.description: Exact match (range_lookup=FALSE) on a horizontal lookup table

HLOOKUP_approximate_match.description: Approximate match (TRUE) requires the first row sorted ascending; returns the largest header <= lookup_value
HLOOKUP_approximate_match.notes: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/hlookup-function-a3034eec-b719-4ba3-bb65-e1ad662ed95f -- approximate match finds the next largest value that is <= lookup_value; 6 falls between 5 and 7, so it matches 5 -> 50

HLOOKUP_not_found_exact_na.description: Exact match with no match found -> #N/A

HLOOKUP_row_index_out_of_range.description: row_index_num greater than the number of rows in table_array -> #REF!

HOUR_of_fractional_day_noon.description: HOUR applied to a bare fractional-day serial (no date part) -- 0.5 of a day is exactly noon

HOUR_of_time_value.description: HOUR applied to a TIME()-constructed value

HOUR_of_date_plus_time_serial.description: HOUR applied to a combined date+time serial extracts only the time-of-day part

HSTACK_two_columns.description: Horizontally join two 2-row columns into a 2x2 array
HSTACK_two_columns.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 1, got '#NAME?'

HYPERLINK_with_friendly_name.description: Formula result (display text) is the friendly_name when supplied

HYPERLINK_without_friendly_name.description: With no friendly_name argument, the displayed result falls back to link_location itself

HYPERLINK_friendly_name_numeric.description: friendly_name may be a number; the formula result is that numeric value

HYPERLINK_friendly_name_from_cell.description: friendly_name supplied via a cell reference

IFERROR_no_error_passthrough.description: No error: original value passes through unchanged

IFERROR_nested_vlookup.description: Catches #N/A bubbling up from a failed VLOOKUP

IFNA_catches_na.description: #N/A from a failed MATCH is caught

IFNA_does_not_catch_other_errors.description: IFNA only catches #N/A; #DIV/0! must pass through unchanged
IFNA_does_not_catch_other_errors.notes: Common compat pitfall: IFNA is NOT a general error trap like IFERROR

IFNA_no_error_passthrough.description: No error: original value passes through

IFS_basic.description: First TRUE condition wins; TRUE literal acts as catch-all

IFS_no_match_no_catchall.description: No condition true and no catch-all -> #N/A

IFS_order_matters.description: Conditions evaluated left-to-right; first match short-circuits
IFS_order_matters.notes: A1>10 is also true but never reached

IFS_catchall_first_value.description: TRUE as the first condition always wins regardless of position

INDEX_whole_column_spill.description: row_num=0 returns the entire column as a spilled array

INDEX_MATCH_combo.description: Classic INDEX/MATCH pattern used as a VLOOKUP replacement

INDIRECT_basic_cell_text.description: Basic ref_text naming a cell literally

INDIRECT_concatenated_ref.description: ref_text built dynamically by concatenating column letter and a row number

INDIRECT_malformed_ref_error.description: A ref_text string that isn't a valid cell reference in either A1 or R1C1 style -> #REF!
INDIRECT_malformed_ref_error.notes: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/indirect-function-474b3a3a-8a26-4f44-b491-92b6306fa261 -- documents that an invalid ref_text returns the #REF! error value

INDIRECT_r1c1_style.description: a1=FALSE switches ref_text parsing to R1C1 notation; R2C1 means row 2, column 1 = A2

INDIRECT_ref_to_blank_cell.description: Referencing a genuinely blank cell returns 0, matching normal cell-reference behavior in a numeric context

INT_basic_positive.description: Positive fractional number truncates its decimal part

INT_negative_rounds_toward_negative_infi.description: Documented classic divergence from TRUNC: INT always rounds DOWN to the nearest integer (toward negative infinity), so a negative fractional number rounds AWAY from zero
INT_negative_rounds_toward_negative_infi.notes: Per Microsoft's INT function docs, INT(-8.9) = -9 because INT rounds down toward negative infinity, unlike TRUNC(-8.9) = -8 which truncates toward zero -- see the paired TRUNC_negative_truncates_toward_zero case in TRUNC.json for the direct comparison

INT_already_integer.description: A whole number is returned unchanged

INT_negative_integer.description: A whole negative number is returned unchanged (no fractional part to drop)

ISBLANK_true_for_genuinely_empty_cell.description: A1 is never written to at all (no setup_cells entry) -- a genuinely empty cell

ISBLANK_false_for_formula_returning_empt.description: A1 contains the formula ="" which displays as nothing but is NOT considered blank -- a classic documented divergence between 'looks empty' and 'is blank'. Verified via https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/help/823838/the-result-is-false-when-you-use-the-isblank-function-in-an-excel-spre ("the result is FALSE even though the cell appears to be empty ... when the cell contains a zero-length string")
ISBLANK_false_for_formula_returning_empt.notes: A1 displays as blank but holds a zero-length-string formula result, not a true blank -- ISBLANK must return FALSE

ISBLANK_false_for_cell_with_value.description: A1 explicitly contains the value 0 -- clearly not blank

ISERR_div0.description: ISERR is TRUE for a #DIV/0! error (any error except #N/A)

ISERR_na.description: Key distinguishing case: ISERR is documented to return FALSE specifically for #N/A, unlike ISERROR (TRUE) -- 'ISERR: Value refers to any error value except #N/A' per https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/is-functions-0f2d7971-6019-40a0-a171-f2d869135665
ISERR_na.notes: This is the whole point of ISERR existing separately from ISERROR: it deliberately excludes #N/A.

ISERR_value_error.description: ISERR is TRUE for a #VALUE! error (not #N/A, so it counts)

ISERROR_div0.description: ISERROR is TRUE for a #DIV/0! error, one of ANY error type it catches (per support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/is-functions-0f2d7971-6019-40a0-a171-f2d869135665)

ISERROR_na.description: ISERROR is also TRUE for #N/A specifically (unlike ISERR, which excludes #N/A) -- contrast with ISERR_na and ISNA_na in the sibling test files

ISERROR_value_error.description: ISERROR is TRUE for a #VALUE! error (text coerced into arithmetic)

ISEVEN_negative_odd.description: A negative odd number -> FALSE (docs' own example is =ISEVEN(-1) -> FALSE)

ISEVEN_noninteger_truncates.description: Non-integer input is truncated toward zero before testing, per Microsoft docs' own example: '=ISEVEN(2.5) ... The decimal portion is truncated, so 2 is tested.' -> TRUE

ISEVEN_zero.description: Zero is documented as even -> TRUE

ISLOGICAL_true.description: TRUE is a logical value -> TRUE

ISLOGICAL_false.description: FALSE is a logical value -> TRUE

ISLOGICAL_number.description: A plain number is not a logical value -> FALSE
ISLOGICAL_number.notes: Even though 1 and TRUE are arithmetically interchangeable, ISLOGICAL distinguishes the underlying boolean type from a number.

ISLOGICAL_text.description: Text is not a logical value -> FALSE
ISLOGICAL_text.notes: The text string "TRUE" is text, not the boolean TRUE.

ISNA_div0.description: Key distinguishing case: ISNA is FALSE for #DIV/0! -- it only recognizes #N/A specifically, per https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/is-functions-0f2d7971-6019-40a0-a171-f2d869135665 ('ISNA: Value refers to the #N/A error value')

ISNA_na.description: ISNA is TRUE only for #N/A, the one case where ISERR disagrees with ISERROR/ISNA

ISNA_value_error.description: ISNA is FALSE for a #VALUE! error (not #N/A)

ISNUMBER_boolean.description: Documented divergence/point of confusion: a logical TRUE/FALSE value is its own distinct type in Excel's IS-function type system and is NOT considered a number, even though it participates in arithmetic as 1/0. Microsoft's IS-functions page (https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/is-functions-0f2d7971-6019-40a0-a171-f2d869135665) states value arguments 'are not converted' and gives ISLOGICAL as the dedicated test for booleans, distinct from ISNUMBER -- the docs do not give an explicit ISNUMBER(TRUE) example, but this is the well-established, widely verified real Excel result.
ISNUMBER_boolean.notes: ISNUMBER(TRUE) = FALSE; use ISLOGICAL to detect booleans instead.; MISMATCH vs expected: expected False, got True

ISODD_noninteger_truncates.description: Non-integer input is truncated toward zero before testing (3.9 -> 3, odd) per https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/isodd-function-1208a56d-4f10-4f44-a5fc-648cafd6c07a ('If number is not an integer, it is truncated.')

ISODD_zero.description: Zero is even, so ISODD(0) -> FALSE

ISOWEEKNUM_doc_example.description: Microsoft's own documented example date/result. Verified via https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/isoweeknum-function-1c2d0afe-d25b-4ab1-8894-8d0520e90e0e

ISOWEEKNUM_prior_iso_year_boundary.description: Genuine algorithmic divergence from plain WEEKNUM: ISO 8601 week 1 is defined as the week containing the year's first Thursday (Monday-start weeks), NOT the week containing Jan 1. Jan 1, 2023 is a Sunday, so it belongs to the week Dec 26, 2022 - Jan 1, 2023, whose Thursday (Dec 29, 2022) is in 2022 -- so this date is ISO week 52 of 2022, even though plain WEEKNUM would call it week 1 of 2023
ISOWEEKNUM_prior_iso_year_boundary.notes: Cross-checked against Python's datetime.date(2023,1,1).isocalendar() -> (2022, 52, 7), which implements ISO 8601

ISOWEEKNUM_same_calendar_date_different_.description: Contrast case: Jan 1, 2024 is a Monday, so (unlike the 2023 case above) it DOES start ISO week 1 of 2024 -- showing the year-boundary quirk depends entirely on which weekday Jan 1 falls on

ISOWEEKNUM_next_iso_year_boundary.description: The same phenomenon at the OTHER end of the year: Dec 31, 2024 is a Tuesday, and the Monday-start week containing it (Dec 30, 2024 - Jan 5, 2025) has its Thursday (Jan 2, 2025) in the following calendar year, so this December date is actually ISO week 1 of 2025

ISTEXT_boolean.description: A logical value is not text -> FALSE

LAMBDA_via_LET.description: LAMBDA bound to a name via LET, then called

LAMBDA_wrong_arg_count.description: Calling with fewer arguments than parameters is an error

LARGE_k1_equals_max.description: k=1 returns the same value as MAX

LARGE_k_out_of_range.description: k larger than the count of values -> #NUM!
LARGE_k_out_of_range.notes: Only 5 values exist; requesting the 6th-largest is out of range; MISMATCH vs expected: expected '#NUM!', got '#VALUE!'

LARGE_k_zero_error.description: k=0 is also out of the valid 1..n range -> #NUM!

LEFT_basic.description: Basic extraction of the first 3 characters

LEFT_num_chars_exceeds_length.description: num_chars larger than the string length returns the whole string, not an error

LEN_numeric_value_coerced.description: A numeric cell value is coerced to text before its digits are counted

LEN_counts_spaces.description: Leading and trailing spaces are counted as characters just like any other character

LET_basic.description: Single binding used in the calculation

LET_chained_bindings.description: Later bindings can reference earlier ones in the same LET

LET_array_binding.description: LET can bind an array literal and pass it to another function

LET_odd_arg_count_error.description: Odd total arg count (missing final calculation expression) is a syntax/value error
LET_odd_arg_count_error.notes: LET requires pairs plus a trailing calculation; here the last pair has no calc term following it in this deliberately malformed call; MISMATCH vs expected: expected '#VALUE!', got '#NAME?'

LN_zero_error.notes: Microsoft's LN docs specify the Number argument must be 'the positive real number for which you want the natural logarithm' -- https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/ln-function-81fe1ed7-dac9-4acd-ba1d-07a142c6118f -- 0 and negative numbers are outside that domain and both are well-established to raise #NUM!, matching every other Excel logarithm function; MISMATCH vs expected: expected '#NUM!', got '#VALUE!'

LN_negative_error.description: Negative numbers are outside LN's domain -> #NUM!

LOG_default_base_ten.description: With base omitted, LOG defaults to base 10
LOG_default_base_ten.notes: Microsoft's LOG docs state 'If base is omitted, it is assumed to be 10' -- https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/log-function-4e82f196-1ca9-4747-8fb0-6c4a3abb3280

LOG_explicit_base.description: Explicit base argument: log base 2 of 8
LOG_explicit_base.notes: This is Microsoft's own documented example: '=LOG(8,2), The result, 3, is the power to which the base must be raised to equal 8' -- https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/log-function-4e82f196-1ca9-4747-8fb0-6c4a3abb3280

LOG_negative_number_error.description: Negative numbers are outside LOG's domain -> #NUM!

LOG10_negative_error.description: Negative numbers are outside LOG10's domain -> #NUM!

LOOKUP_vector_exact_match.description: Vector form exact match against an ascending-sorted lookup_vector
LOOKUP_vector_exact_match.notes: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/lookup-function-446d94af-663b-451d-8251-369d5e3864cb -- lookup_vector must be in ascending order for LOOKUP to work correctly; this vector is sorted

LOOKUP_vector_approximate_between_values.description: No exact match; LOOKUP uses the largest lookup_vector value that is <= lookup_value
LOOKUP_vector_approximate_between_values.notes: 3.5 has no exact match; largest value <=3.5 is 3 -> paired result 30

LOOKUP_below_range_na.description: lookup_value smaller than every entry in lookup_vector -> #N/A

LOOKUP_array_form_more_rows_than_cols.description: Array form (single 2-D argument, no separate result_vector): since A1:B3 has more rows than columns, LOOKUP searches the FIRST column and returns from the LAST column
LOOKUP_array_form_more_rows_than_cols.notes: Per Microsoft docs' array-form rule (searches first row/col depending on which is longer): match on A2=2 returns paired last-column value 200

LOWER_leaves_numbers_and_symbols.description: Digits and symbols have no case and are left unchanged

MAKEARRAY_rc.description: Build a 2x2 array where each cell = row*col
MAKEARRAY_rc.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 1, got '#NAME?'

MAP_double.description: Apply a lambda to each element (double it)
MAP_double.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 2, got '#NAME?'

MATCH_approx_descending.description: match_type -1: smallest value >= lookup, descending sorted data
MATCH_approx_descending.notes: Position 3 holds value 4, the smallest value >= 3 in descending data

MEDIAN_single_value.description: Degenerate single-value case returns that value

MID_start_beyond_length.description: start_num beyond the string length returns an empty string, not an error

MID_num_chars_exceeds_remaining.description: num_chars larger than the number of characters remaining returns just what's left, not an error or padded result

MIN_basic.description: Smallest value in a simple numeric range

MIN_ignores_text.description: Text values in the range are ignored, not treated as 0

MIN_empty_range.description: A range with no numeric values at all returns 0
MIN_empty_range.notes: MIN of an empty/all-blank range is documented to return 0

MINIFS_single_criteria.description: Min of A where paired B > 2

MINIFS_no_matches.description: No rows satisfy the criteria -> 0 (not an error)

MINUTE_of_time_value.description: MINUTE applied to a TIME()-constructed value

MINUTE_of_time_near_midnight.description: MINUTE just before a day boundary

MOD_negative_divisor.description: Negative divisor: result takes the sign of the divisor

MODE_basic_repeated_value.description: Basic case with one clearly most-frequent value

MODE_negative_numbers_repeated.description: MODE works the same way on negative numbers

MODE_ignores_text_and_blanks.description: Text values and blank cells in the range are ignored when finding the mode

MONTH_of_date.description: MONTH applied to a constructed date

MROUND_basic.description: Rounds 10 to the nearest multiple of 3. Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/mround-function-c299c3b0-15a5-426d-aa4b-d2d5b3baf427 (worked example)

MROUND_both_negative.description: Both arguments negative (same sign, as required) -> rounds to nearest multiple, sign preserved. Source: Microsoft MROUND docs worked example.

MROUND_fractional_multiple.description: Rounds 1.3 to the nearest multiple of the fractional value 0.2. Source: Microsoft MROUND docs worked example.

NA_direct.description: NA() returns the #N/A error value itself

NOT_text_error.description: An arbitrary text literal cannot be coerced to a logical value -> #VALUE!

NUMBERVALUE_malformed_text.description: Text that cannot be parsed as a number at all -> #VALUE!

NUMBERVALUE_decimal_separator_used_twice.description: A decimal separator appearing more than once in the text is documented as invalid input -> #VALUE!
NUMBERVALUE_decimal_separator_used_twice.notes: Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/numbervalue-function-1b05c8cf-2bfa-4437-af70-596c7ea7d879 - decimal separator used multiple times is explicitly called out as an error condition

OFFSET_basic_single_cell.description: Move 2 rows down and 1 column right from A1, landing on B3
