OR_range_numeric_all_zero.description: A range of all-zero numbers coerces to all-FALSE -> FALSE

OR_range_text_ignored.description: Text within a range argument is ignored (not coerced, not an error), per https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/or-function-7d17ad14-8700-4281-b308-00b131e22af0: 'If an array or reference argument contains text or empty cells, those values are ignored.'

PERCENTILE.EXC_k_zero_error.description: Documented divergence from PERCENTILE.INC: PERCENTILE.EXC requires k strictly between 0 and 1 (and more precisely between 1/(n+1) and n/(n+1)); k=0 -> #NUM!. Confirmed via Microsoft's PERCENTILE.EXC docs (https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/percentile-exc-function-bbaa7204-e9e1-4010-85bf-c31dc5dce4ba): "If k is <=0 or if k >=1, PERCENTILE.EXC returns the #NUM! error value." PERCENTILE.INC_k_zero on identical data returns 1, not an error.

PERCENTILE.EXC_k_one_error.description: k=1 is likewise out of PERCENTILE.EXC's exclusive valid range -> #NUM!, whereas PERCENTILE.INC_k_one on identical data returns 10

PERCENTILE.EXC_k_half.description: k=0.5 is within the valid range for n=10 (1/11 < 0.5 < 10/11) and matches PERCENTILE.INC's result at the median

PERCENTILE.EXC_k_near_lower_bound.notes: pos=k*(n+1)=0.1*11=1.1 -> interpolate 10% of the way from data[0]=1 to data[1]=2 -> 1.1

PERCENTILE.INC_k_zero.description: k=0 is a valid boundary for PERCENTILE.INC and returns the minimum

PERCENTILE.INC_k_one.description: k=1 is a valid boundary for PERCENTILE.INC and returns the maximum

PERCENTILE.INC_k_half.description: k=0.5 (median); compare to PERCENTILE.EXC_k_half on identical data

PI_value.description: PI() takes no arguments and returns the double-precision approximation of pi

PI_rounded_four_places.description: Common real-world usage: rounding PI() to a fixed number of decimals

PI_times_two.description: PI() used directly in arithmetic (e.g. computing a full turn in radians)

POWER_negative_exponent.description: Negative exponent gives a reciprocal power

POWER_fractional_exponent_positive_base.description: Fractional exponent on a positive base is a well-defined real root (square root here)

POWER_negative_base_fractional_exponent.description: A negative base raised to a non-integer exponent has no real-number result in general (would require complex numbers), so Excel errors
POWER_negative_base_fractional_exponent.notes: Confirmed via Microsoft's own community support: entering =(-8)^(2/3) (equivalent power semantics to POWER) returns #NUM! in Excel -- https://answers.microsoft.com/en-us/msoffice/forum/all/excel-calculating-exponential-of-negative-number/5c67eae2-5682-4daf-b3d6-50b22e9919c2 -- note that -8^(1/3) is mathematically -2 as a real cube root, but Excel's POWER/^ implementation does not special-case rational exponents with odd integer denominators and returns #NUM! for any negative base with a non-integer exponent; MISMATCH vs expected: expected '#NUM!', got -2

POWER_zero_to_zero.description: 0 raised to the 0th power; by the near-universal 'empty product' convention this is 1 (matches VBA's own '0^0', Python, C#, and LibreOffice/OpenOffice Calc)
POWER_zero_to_zero.notes: Real Excel (the worksheet function, and even the '^' operator) has a long-standing, Microsoft-acknowledged bug -- reproduced in Excel 2007 and 2010 and still reported unresolved -- where both '=0^0' and '=POWER(0,0)' incorrectly return #NUM! instead of 1, even though VBA's own 'x = 0 ^ 0' correctly evaluates to 1 in the same product. Recording the spec/cross-engine-consensus value (1) as 'expected' here so this exact Excel quirk shows up as a flagged divergence rather than being silently baked in as 'correct' -- https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/answers/questions/4850441/excel-formula-bug-00-gives-num

PROPER_basic_sentence.description: Basic multi-word capitalization: the first letter of each word is capitalized, the rest lowercased

PROPER_digit_letter_boundary.description: Microsoft's own documented example: PROPER treats any non-letter (including a run of digits) as a word boundary, so the letter immediately following the digits is also capitalized and treated as a new word-start

PROPER_apostrophe_word_boundary.description: PROPER treats an apostrophe as a word boundary too, capitalizing the letter that follows it -- so a name like o'brien becomes O'Brien. Widely reproduced/documented real PROPER behavior (the same non-letter-is-a-boundary rule Microsoft documents for the digit case above), though this exact example isn't on Microsoft's own reference page

QUARTILE.INC_quart_two_median.description: quart=2 returns the median, equal to PERCENTILE.INC(...,0.5)

QUARTILE.INC_quart_out_of_range.description: quart must be an integer 0-4; 5 is out of range -> #NUM!

RADIANS_negative.description: Negative degrees convert to negative radians

RAND_exists_and_in_unit_range.description: Existence probe only: RAND() is non-deterministic; we just confirm the engine recognizes the function (not #NAME?) and returns a plausible value
RAND_exists_and_in_unit_range.notes: Non-deterministic by design; harness should only check 0<=value<1 and absence of #NAME?

RANDBETWEEN_exists_bounds.description: Existence probe: confirm recognized and result plausible (integer 1..10)
RANDBETWEEN_exists_bounds.notes: Non-deterministic by design; harness should only check 1<=value<=10 and absence of #NAME?

RANDBETWEEN_degenerate_equal_bounds.description: When bottom==top the result is deterministic

RANDBETWEEN_negative_range.description: Existence probe with an all-negative range

RANK.AVG_tie_averages_ranks.description: Documented divergence from RANK.EQ: tied values get the AVERAGE of the ranks they would occupy, not the top rank. Per Microsoft's RANK.AVG docs (https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/rank-avg-function-bd406a6f-eb38-4d73-aa8e-6d1c3c72e83a): "If more than one value has the same rank, the average rank is returned."
RANK.AVG_tie_averages_ranks.notes: The two tied 20s would occupy ranks 3 and 4 (descending); average = (3+4)/2 = 3.5, vs RANK.EQ's 3 on identical data

RANK.AVG_no_tie_matches_RANK_EQ.description: With no ties, RANK.AVG matches RANK.EQ exactly

RANK.AVG_tie_ascending_order.description: Tie-averaging also applies with the ascending order argument
RANK.AVG_tie_ascending_order.notes: Ascending, the two tied 20s would occupy ranks 2 and 3 (only 10 is smaller); average = 2.5

RANK.EQ_basic_descending.description: RANK.EQ is the modern equivalent of RANK; identical data/formula to RANK_basic_descending must give the identical result

RANK.EQ_ascending_order.description: Nonzero order argument ranks ascending (smallest=1), matching RANK's behavior

RANK.EQ_tie_shares_top_rank.description: Like legacy RANK, ties get the same top rank (contrast with RANK.AVG's averaged rank on identical data, see RANK.AVG_tie_averages_ranks)

RANK_basic_descending.notes: Only 40 is greater than 30, so 30 is rank 2

RANK_ascending_order.notes: 10,20,20 are all less than 30, so 30 is rank 4 ascending

RANK_tie_shares_top_rank.description: Tied values share the same (best/top) rank
RANK_tie_shares_top_rank.notes: Two values (30,40) exceed 20, so both 20s tie for rank 3; rank 4 is skipped entirely

REDUCE_sum.description: Accumulate a range into a single value (sum)

REPLACE_basic_by_position.description: Replaces the first 3 characters by position, regardless of what they are

REPLACE_position_vs_content_contrast.description: REPLACE targets characters purely by position (start_num/num_chars), unlike SUBSTITUTE which matches by content: here characters 2-4 ('ana') are removed and replaced with 'XYZ' regardless of what they spell
REPLACE_position_vs_content_contrast.notes: 'banana' = b(1)a(2)n(3)a(4)n(5)a(6); removing positions 2-4 ('ana') and inserting 'XYZ' gives 'b' + 'XYZ' + 'na' = 'bXYZna'

REPLACE_larger_range.description: Replaces a 5-character mid-string range (positions 6-10) with a single character

REPT_zero_times.description: number_times=0 returns an empty string, not an error
REPT_zero_times.notes: Microsoft docs: "If number_times is 0 (zero), REPT returns \"\" (empty text)." Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/rept-function-04c4d778-e712-43b4-9c15-d656582bb061

REPT_negative_times.notes: Documented behavior: REPT does not accept negative number_times and returns #VALUE! (per Microsoft's REPT reference and its documented remarks on the number_times argument)

REPT_fractional_truncated.description: A non-integer number_times is truncated toward zero before repeating
REPT_fractional_truncated.notes: Documented: "If number_times is not an integer, it is truncated."

RIGHT_basic.description: Basic extraction of the last 3 characters

RIGHT_num_chars_exceeds_length.description: num_chars larger than the string length returns the whole string, not an error

ROUND_half_away_from_zero.description: Excel/LO ROUND uses arithmetic rounding (half away from zero), NOT banker's rounding (round-half-to-even)
ROUND_half_away_from_zero.notes: Banker's rounding would give 2; correct spreadsheet behavior is 3

ROUND_negative_half_away_from_zero.description: Negative half-values also round away from zero

ROUND_negative_digits.description: Negative num_digits rounds to the left of the decimal point

ROUND_float_representation_quirk.description: Tests whether the engine falls into the naive-binary-float trap: the IEEE-754 double closest to 1.005 is actually ~1.00499999999999989, so a naive floor(x*100+0.5)/100 implementation would round DOWN to 1.00
ROUND_float_representation_quirk.notes: Both Excel and LibreOffice correctly return 1.01, not 1.0 -- they normalize to ~15 significant decimal digits before rounding, specifically to avoid this well-known binary-float pitfall

ROUNDDOWN_basic_positive.description: Always rounds toward zero, regardless of the discarded digit

ROUNDDOWN_negative_number.description: For negative numbers, 'down' means toward zero (less negative), not toward negative infinity

ROUNDDOWN_negative_num_digits.description: Negative num_digits rounds to the left of the decimal point (nearest multiple of 10 here)
ROUNDDOWN_negative_num_digits.notes: Rounding 21.5 down (toward zero) to the nearest 10 gives 20 -- this is Microsoft's own documented example for ROUNDDOWN, and the direct counterpart to ROUNDUP(21.5,-1)=30

ROUNDDOWN_decimal_places.description: Rounds down to 2 decimal places, discarding the remaining digits

ROUNDUP_basic_positive.description: Always rounds away from zero, regardless of the discarded digit

ROUNDUP_negative_number.description: For negative numbers, 'up' means away from zero (more negative), not toward positive infinity

ROUNDUP_negative_num_digits.description: Negative num_digits rounds to the left of the decimal point (nearest multiple of 10 here)
ROUNDUP_negative_num_digits.notes: Rounding 21.5 up (away from zero) to the nearest 10 gives 30 -- this is Microsoft's own documented example for ROUNDUP

ROUNDUP_decimal_places.description: Rounds up to 2 decimal places even though the 3rd decimal (1) would round down normally

ROW_no_arg_uses_formula_cell.description: No argument: ROW() returns the row of the cell containing the formula itself. This case's formula lands in cell F1 by default, which is row 1
ROW_no_arg_uses_formula_cell.notes: The harness writes each case's formula into cell F1 (row 1, column 6); ROW() with no anchor argument returns the formula's own row, i.e. 1

ROW_explicit_cell_ref.description: Explicit single-cell reference argument overrides the formula's own location

ROW_range_returns_first_row.description: In non-array (scalar) context, ROW() of a multi-row range returns the row number of the FIRST row of the reference

ROW_far_cell.description: Sanity check with a larger row number

ROWS_basic_column_range.description: Height (row count) of a single-column range; does not depend on cell contents

ROWS_single_cell.description: A single cell reference has exactly 1 row

ROWS_2d_range.description: Row count of a 2-D range only counts rows, ignoring the 3 columns

SCAN_running_sum.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 1, got '#NAME?'

SEARCH_case_insensitive_match.description: SEARCH is case-insensitive: lowercase 'a' matches the leading uppercase 'A' -> position 1 (contrast with FIND on this exact same input, which is case-sensitive and returns #VALUE!)

SEARCH_wildcard_asterisk.description: SEARCH supports wildcards unlike FIND: '*' matches any sequence of characters, so "p*e" matches the substring "pple" starting at position 2
SEARCH_wildcard_asterisk.notes: Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/search-function-9ab04538-0e55-4719-a72e-b6f54513b495 - SEARCH allows the wildcard characters '?' and '*' in find_text; FIND does not support wildcards at all

SEARCH_wildcard_question_mark.description: '?' wildcard matches exactly one character, so "?pple" matches "Apple" starting at position 1

SEARCH_not_found.description: find_text not present anywhere in within_text -> #VALUE!

SECOND_of_fractional_day_noon.description: SECOND applied to a bare fractional-day serial -- exactly noon has 0 seconds past the minute

SECOND_of_time_value.description: SECOND applied to a TIME()-constructed value

SECOND_of_time_near_midnight.description: SECOND just before a day boundary

SEQUENCE_basic_vertical.description: Default rows-only sequence starting at 1, step 1
SEQUENCE_basic_vertical.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 1, got '#NAME?'

SEQUENCE_rows_cols.description: 2 rows x 3 columns filled row-major from 1
SEQUENCE_rows_cols.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 1, got '#NAME?'

SEQUENCE_start_step.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 10, got '#NAME?'

SEQUENCE_negative_step.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 5, got '#NAME?'

SIGN_zero.description: Zero returns 0, not an error -- SIGN never errors on a numeric input

SIGN_small_negative_fraction.description: Any negative value, no matter how small, still returns -1

SMALL_k1_equals_min.description: k=1 returns the same value as MIN

SMALL_k_out_of_range.description: k larger than the count of values -> #NUM!
SMALL_k_out_of_range.notes: Only 5 values exist; requesting the 6th-smallest is out of range; MISMATCH vs expected: expected '#NUM!', got '#VALUE!'

SMALL_k_zero_error.description: k=0 is also out of the valid 1..n range -> #NUM!

SORT_default_ascending.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 1, got '#NAME?'

SORT_descending.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 5, got '#NAME?'

SORT_by_second_column.description: 2D range sorted by its second column ascending
SORT_by_second_column.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 'y', got '#NAME?'

SORT_empty_range_error.description: Sorting a single blank cell (degenerate case)
SORT_empty_range_error.notes: Blank cell sorts as 0 in numeric context; no error expected; MISMATCH vs expected: expected 0, got '#NAME?'

SORTBY_by_other_col.description: Sort column A by the values in column B (ascending)
SORTBY_by_other_col.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 'y', got '#NAME?'

SQRT_zero.description: Square root of zero is zero

SQRT_negative_returns_num_error.description: Negative input has no real square root -> #NUM!
SQRT_negative_returns_num_error.notes: Microsoft's SQRT docs state verbatim: 'If number is negative, SQRT returns the #NUM! error value' -- https://support.microsoft.com/en-US/Excel/functions/sqrt-function; MISMATCH vs expected: expected '#NUM!', got '#VALUE!'

SQRT_documented_abs_workaround.description: Microsoft's own SQRT documentation demonstrates wrapping a possibly-negative argument in ABS as the standard workaround to avoid #NUM!
SQRT_documented_abs_workaround.notes: Same doc page above shows an example using '=SQRT(ABS(A2))' specifically to sidestep the negative-input #NUM! case

STDEV.P_diverges_from_sample_same_data.description: Population standard deviation on the SAME dataset {2,4,4,4,5,5,7,9} used for STDEV/STDEV.S - divides by n instead of n-1, giving a smaller, exact result (2) vs the sample stdev's 2.138089935299395

STDEV.P_two_points_diverges.description: Same two-point data as the sample-stdev cases (10,20) but population formula divides by n=2 instead of n-1=1, giving a much smaller result
STDEV.P_two_points_diverges.notes: Population variance=((10-15)^2+(20-15)^2)/2=25; sqrt(25)=5, vs sample stdev's 7.0710678118654755 on identical data

STDEV.P_single_value_no_error.description: Unlike STDEV.S/STDEV, a single value does NOT error for the population formula, since it divides by n (=1), not n-1
STDEV.P_single_value_no_error.notes: Population variance of one point = 0/1 = 0, a valid result - genuine divergence from STDEV.S's #DIV/0! on the same single-cell input

STDEV.S_basic_two_points.description: Sample standard deviation of two hand-verifiable values, identical formula/data to STDEV_basic_two_points in the STDEV file

STDEV.S_matches_STDEV_same_data.description: STDEV.S on the classic Wikipedia sample dataset {2,4,4,4,5,5,7,9} - must match STDEV_matches_STDEV_S_same_data exactly, since STDEV.S is simply the Excel-2010+ modern name for the legacy STDEV calculation (same n-1 sample formula)

STDEV.S_single_value_div0.description: Sample stdev needs n>=2 (divides by n-1); one value -> #DIV/0!, confirmed against Microsoft's STDEV.S documentation of the n-1 method (https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/stdev-s-function-7d69cf97-0c1f-4acf-be27-f3e83904cc23)

STDEV_basic_two_points.description: Sample standard deviation of two hand-verifiable values

STDEV_matches_STDEV_S_same_data.description: Legacy STDEV on the classic Wikipedia sample dataset {2,4,4,4,5,5,7,9}; compare to STDEV.S_matches_STDEV_same_data on identical data in the STDEV.S file - both must return exactly the same value since STDEV.S is just the modern name for the same sample-stdev calculation

STDEV_single_value_div0.description: Sample stdev needs at least 2 data points (divides by n-1); a single value -> #DIV/0!
STDEV_single_value_div0.notes: n-1=0 with a single supplied value, causing division by zero

SUBSTITUTE_all_occurrences.description: Without instance_num, every occurrence of old_text is replaced

SUBSTITUTE_instance_num.description: instance_num=2 replaces only the 2nd occurrence of old_text, leaving the 1st untouched

SUBSTITUTE_no_match.description: old_text not present in text returns the text unchanged

SUBSTITUTE_case_sensitive.description: SUBSTITUTE matches old_text case-sensitively; lowercase 'a' does not match the uppercase 'A' in "Apple", so the text is returned unchanged

SUM_basic_range.description: Basic sum of a contiguous numeric range

SUM_text_in_cell_reference_ignored.description: A text value stored in a referenced cell is IGNORED by SUM, not coerced to a number, even though it looks numeric
SUM_text_in_cell_reference_ignored.notes: A2 holds the text string '2' (not the number 2); SUM over a range skips text cells entirely, so the total is 1+3=4, not 6

SUM_literal_numeric_string_argument_coer.description: A numeric-looking TEXT LITERAL passed directly as a function argument (not via a cell reference) IS coerced to a number, unlike the same text sitting in a referenced cell
SUM_literal_numeric_string_argument_coer.notes: Contrast with SUM_text_in_cell_reference_ignored: Excel coerces literal string arguments that look like numbers when they are typed directly into the formula, but does not do so for text found inside a range reference; MISMATCH vs expected: expected 6, got '#VALUE!'

SUM_empty_range.description: Summing an entirely empty range returns 0, not an error

SUMIF_basic.description: Sum of cells in the range itself that satisfy the criteria (no sum_range)

SUMIF_separate_sum_range.description: Criteria evaluated against A but the matching B cells are summed
SUMIF_separate_sum_range.notes: Rows 3,4,5 satisfy A>2; sum of paired B = 30+40+50

SUMIF_wildcard_text.description: Wildcard text criteria matches cells starting with 'a'

SUMIF_blank_cells_ignored.description: Blank cells interspersed in the range are simply skipped, not treated as matching or as errors
SUMIF_blank_cells_ignored.notes: A2 and A4 are blank (unset); only A1,A3,A5 exist and all satisfy >0

SUMIFS_basic.description: Sum of A where paired B > 2

SUMIFS_wildcard.description: Wildcard text criteria matches 2 of 3 rows

SUMPRODUCT_basic.description: Sum of pairwise products across two equal-sized arrays

SUMPRODUCT_single_array_acts_like_sum.description: With only one array argument, SUMPRODUCT just sums its elements

SUMPRODUCT_boolean_double_negative_count.description: Classic double-unary-minus idiom coerces a boolean array to 1/0 to count matches without an extra helper column
SUMPRODUCT_boolean_double_negative_count.notes: A3,A4,A5 satisfy >2; --(...) turns TRUE/FALSE into 1/0 and SUMPRODUCT sums them

SUMPRODUCT_condition_times_values_sumif_.description: Multiplying a boolean condition array by a value array replicates SUMIF without a separate criteria function
SUMPRODUCT_condition_times_values_sumif_.notes: Rows where A>2 (rows 3,4,5) contribute their B value: 30+40+50=120

SUMPRODUCT_mismatched_array_sizes_error.description: Arrays of different dimensions cannot be multiplied element-wise -> #VALUE!
SUMPRODUCT_mismatched_array_sizes_error.notes: Microsoft docs: array arguments must have the same dimensions, otherwise SUMPRODUCT returns #VALUE!

SWITCH_default_value.description: Trailing unpaired argument acts as the default

TAKE_first_2x2.description: Take the first 2 rows and first 2 columns of a 3x3 range
TAKE_first_2x2.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 1, got '#NAME?'

TEXT_currency_format.description: Currency-style format code with thousands separator and 2 decimal places, rounding the value. Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/text-function-20d5ac4d-7b94-49fd-bb38-93d29371225c (worked example)

TEXT_percentage_format.description: Percentage format code multiplies by 100, appends %, and rounds to 1 decimal place. Source: Microsoft TEXT docs worked example.

TEXT_scientific_format.description: Scientific-notation format code. Source: Microsoft TEXT docs worked example.

TEXT_zero_padding.description: Zero-padding format code pads with leading zeros to fill every '0' placeholder. Source: Microsoft TEXT docs worked example.

TEXT_iso_date_format.description: ISO-style date format code applied to a fixed, deterministic date (not TODAY()) so the result is reproducible.

TEXT_weekday_name_format.description: Full weekday name format code. March 5, 2024 falls on a Tuesday (verified against a real calendar).

TEXTAFTER_basic.description: Text after the first delimiter occurrence

TEXTAFTER_negative_instance.description: Negative instance_num counts delimiters from the end
TEXTAFTER_negative_instance.notes: -1 means the last delimiter; text after it is 'c'; MISMATCH vs expected: expected 'c', got '#NAME?'

TEXTAFTER_not_found_custom.description: Delimiter not present with if_not_found supplied

TEXTBEFORE_basic.description: Text before the first delimiter occurrence

TEXTBEFORE_not_found_default.description: Delimiter not present and no if_not_found -> #N/A

TEXTBEFORE_not_found_custom.description: Delimiter not present with if_not_found supplied

TEXTBEFORE_negative_instance.description: Negative instance_num counts delimiters from the end
TEXTBEFORE_negative_instance.notes: -1 means the last delimiter; text before it is 'a-b'; MISMATCH vs expected: expected 'a-b', got '#NAME?'

TEXTJOIN_ignore_empty_false.description: ignore_empty=FALSE keeps the empty string as a segment

TEXTJOIN_range.description: Joining a cell range with a delimiter

TEXTJOIN_multiple_ranges.description: Multiple ranges are flattened and joined in order

TEXTSPLIT_basic.description: Basic column split on a single delimiter
TEXTSPLIT_basic.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 'a', got '#NAME?'

TEXTSPLIT_row_and_col_delims.description: col_delimiter="," and row_delimiter=";" produce a 2x2 grid
TEXTSPLIT_row_and_col_delims.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 'a', got '#NAME?'

TEXTSPLIT_ignore_empty.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 'a', got '#NAME?'

TEXTSPLIT_pad_with.description: Uneven row lengths are padded with pad_with instead of #N/A
TEXTSPLIT_pad_with.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 'a', got '#NAME?'

TIME_basic.description: Ordinary in-range h/m/s construction returns the fraction-of-a-day serial

TIME_hour_wraps_modulo_24.description: Documented behavior: an hour argument greater than 23 wraps modulo 24 instead of erroring or accumulating past a single day. Verified via https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/time-function-9a5aff99-8f7d-4611-845e-747d0b8d5457 ("Any value greater than 23 will be divided by 24 and the remainder will be treated as the hour value")

TIME_doc_example_27_hours.description: The exact example given in Microsoft's own TIME documentation ("TIME(27,0,0) = TIME(3,0,0) = .125 or 3:00 AM")
TIME_doc_example_27_hours.notes: 27 mod 24 = 3 -> TIME(3,0,0) = 3/24 = 0.125, matching the cited doc example verbatim

TOCOL_flatten_by_row.description: Flatten a 2x2 range into a single column, scanning by row
TOCOL_flatten_by_row.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 1, got '#NAME?'

TODAY_exists_and_plausible.description: Existence probe only: TODAY() is non-deterministic (changes daily), so there is no fixed 'expected' value
TODAY_exists_and_plausible.notes: Non-deterministic by design; harness/human should only check the engine recognizes the function (not #NAME?/#VALUE!) and returns a plausible large positive integer serial

TODAY_year_is_plausible.description: Wrapping TODAY() in YEAR() gives a coarser but still non-deterministic probe
TODAY_year_is_plausible.notes: Non-deterministic; harness/human should only check the result is a plausible 4-digit year (>= 2026 as of this test's authoring)

TODAY_self_difference_is_deterministic.description: Even though TODAY() itself is volatile, two calls within the SAME recalculation pass must return the identical value, so their difference is always exactly 0 -- a genuinely deterministic assertion about a volatile function
TODAY_self_difference_is_deterministic.notes: Both TODAY() calls are evaluated as part of one recalculation of one cell, so they cannot differ

TOROW_flatten_by_row.description: Flatten a 2x2 range into a single row, scanning by row
TOROW_flatten_by_row.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 1, got '#NAME?'

TRANSPOSE_row_to_column.description: 1x3 horizontal row transposed into a 3x1 vertical column

TRANSPOSE_2x3_to_3x2.description: 2 rows x 3 columns transposed into 3 rows x 2 columns

TRANSPOSE_single_cell_degenerate.description: Degenerate 1x1 case: transposing a single cell yields the same single value

TRANSPOSE_blank_cell_becomes_zero.description: A blank source cell transposes to 0, not an empty string, matching normal blank-cell-in-formula behavior
TRANSPOSE_blank_cell_becomes_zero.notes: B1 is left unset (blank); its transposed position reads back as 0

TRIM_collapse_and_strip.description: Leading/trailing spaces are stripped and multiple interior spaces collapse to a single space

TRIM_tab_not_removed.description: TRIM only removes the regular ASCII space character (decimal 32); it does NOT remove other whitespace such as a tab (CHAR(9)), so the leading tab survives untouched
TRIM_tab_not_removed.notes: Microsoft docs: "the TRIM function was designed to trim the 7-bit ASCII space character (value 32) from text" -- it does not generalize to other whitespace. Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/trim-function-410388fa-c5df-49c6-b16c-9e5630b479f9

TRIM_nonbreaking_space_not_removed.description: TRIM does not remove the non-breaking space character (CHAR(160), HTML  ) even though it renders as whitespace -- a real, documented, and easy-to-miss cross-engine divergence point since data pasted from web pages is full of CHAR(160)
TRIM_nonbreaking_space_not_removed.notes: Microsoft docs state explicitly: "by itself, the TRIM function does not remove the nonbreaking space character (which has a decimal value of 160 and is commonly used in web pages as the HTML entity  )." Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/trim-function-410388fa-c5df-49c6-b16c-9e5630b479f9; MISMATCH vs expected: expected '\xa0Hello\xa0', got '�Hello�'

TRIM_single_spaces_untouched.description: A string with only single spaces between words passes through unchanged

TRUE_zero_arg_function.description: The zero-argument function form TRUE() is callable and returns the logical value TRUE, per https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/true-function-7652c6e3-8987-48d0-97cd-ef223246b3fb

TRUE_equals_keyword.description: TRUE() returns a genuine logical value equal to the bare TRUE keyword

TRUE_in_if.description: TRUE() used as a condition behaves exactly like the TRUE keyword

TRUNC_basic_positive.description: Positive fractional number truncates its decimal part (same result as INT here)

TRUNC_negative_truncates_toward_zero.description: Documented classic divergence from INT: TRUNC simply removes the fractional part with no rounding, i.e. truncates TOWARD zero
TRUNC_negative_truncates_toward_zero.notes: Per Microsoft's TRUNC function docs, TRUNC(-8.9) = -8 (fractional part discarded, toward zero), whereas INT(-8.9) = -9 (rounds down, away from zero) -- see the paired INT_negative_rounds_toward_negative_infinity case in INT.json

TRUNC_num_digits_positive.description: num_digits=2 keeps two decimal places, discarding the rest without rounding
TRUNC_num_digits_positive.notes: 3.14159 truncated (not rounded) at 2 decimals is 3.14; if it were ROUND instead, 3.14159 would still round to 3.14 here, but TRUNC(3.145,2) vs ROUND(3.145,2) would diverge -- this case isolates the num_digits mechanic, not the rounding-vs-truncating distinction

TRUNC_negative_num_digits.description: Negative num_digits truncates digits to the LEFT of the decimal point
TRUNC_negative_num_digits.notes: Negative num_digits zeroes out the last 2 digits of the integer part without any rounding

TYPE_error.description: An error value -> code 16. TYPE classifies the error's TYPE rather than propagating the error itself, matching ISERROR-style special-casing

UNICHAR_accented_char.description: Basic code point beyond the 7-bit ASCII/basic-Latin range (U+00E9 'e with acute')

UNICHAR_roundtrip_via_unicode.description: Round trip: UNICODE() extracts the code point of an accented character, UNICHAR() converts it straight back
UNICHAR_roundtrip_via_unicode.notes: UNICODE(char) then UNICHAR(that number) must reproduce the original character exactly

UNICHAR_astral_emoji.description: Code point beyond the Basic Multilingual Plane (U+1F600 GRINNING FACE emoji), requiring a UTF-16 surrogate pair internally
UNICHAR_astral_emoji.notes: 128512 decimal = U+1F600, the grinning-face emoji; modern Excel supports supplementary-plane code points via surrogate pairs

UNICHAR_zero_error.description: UNICHAR(0) is documented to raise #VALUE! (0 is not a valid character code point). Verified via https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/unichar-function-ffeb64f5-f131-44c6-b332-5cd72f0659b8 ("Number: ... Numbers <=0 ... UNICHAR returns the #VALUE! error value")

UNICHAR_negative_error.description: Negative code point is out of range -> #VALUE! per the same UNICHAR documentation as UNICHAR_zero_error

UNICODE_accented_char.description: Code point of an accented character beyond basic Latin (U+00E9)

UNICODE_astral_emoji.description: Code point of a supplementary-plane emoji character (U+1F600), the inverse of the UNICHAR_astral_emoji case

UNICODE_empty_string_error.description: Empty text has no first character to inspect -> #VALUE!. Verified via https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/unicode-function-adb74aaa-a2a5-4dde-aff6-966e4e81f16f (documented error handling: invalid/partial character data returns #VALUE!)

UNIQUE_basic.description: Basic unique list preserving first-occurrence order
UNIQUE_basic.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 1, got '#NAME?'

UNIQUE_exactly_once.description: exactly_once=TRUE returns only items that appear exactly once
UNIQUE_exactly_once.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 3, got '#NAME?'

UNIQUE_by_column.description: by_col=TRUE compares whole columns instead of rows
UNIQUE_by_column.notes: Column A and column C are identical (1,1); unique columns are {1,2} and {1,1} but by_col dedups the repeated column, leaving 2 columns: [1,2] and [1,1] -> flatten to first row [1,2]; MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 1, got '#NAME?'

UNIQUE_no_duplicates.description: Input with no duplicates returns the input unchanged
UNIQUE_no_duplicates.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 5, got '#NAME?'

UPPER_leaves_numbers_and_symbols.description: Digits and symbols have no case and are left unchanged

VALUE_numeric_string.description: Basic numeric-looking text string converts to a number

VALUE_percentage_string.description: A percentage-formatted text string converts to its underlying decimal, since Excel stores percentages internally as decimals (50% == 0.5)

VALUE_currency_string.description: Currency formatting (symbol + thousands separator) is stripped during conversion; matches Microsoft's own VALUE documentation example

VALUE_non_numeric_string_error.description: A string that isn't a recognized number/date/time format -> #VALUE!

VAR.S_basic_two_points.description: Sample variance of two hand-verifiable values

VAR.S_wikipedia_dataset.description: Sample variance on the classic {2,4,4,4,5,5,7,9} dataset; this is the square of STDEV.S_matches_STDEV_same_data's result (2.138089935299395^2)

VAR.S_single_value_div0.description: Sample variance needs n>=2 (divides by n-1); one value -> #DIV/0!

VLOOKUP_approximate_match.description: Approximate match requires ascending sorted first column
VLOOKUP_approximate_match.notes: 3 is between 2 and 4; approx match returns the row for the largest value <= lookup (2 -> 20)

VLOOKUP_col_index_out_of_range.description: col_index_num beyond the table width -> #REF! per Microsoft docs
VLOOKUP_col_index_out_of_range.notes: Microsoft docs specify #REF! for out-of-range col_index_num; record engines' ACTUAL error code here since this is a known point of cross-engine divergence; MISMATCH vs expected: expected '#REF!', got '#VALUE!'

VLOOKUP_wildcard_exact_mode.description: Wildcards are honored even in exact-match mode
VLOOKUP_wildcard_exact_mode.notes: First row matching the a* wildcard pattern is 'apple'

VSTACK_two_rows.description: Vertically stack two 2-col rows into a 2x2 array
VSTACK_two_rows.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 1, got '#NAME?'

WEEKDAY_default_type1.description: Default return_type 1: Sunday=1..Saturday=7. Feb 14, 2008 is a Thursday. Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/weekday-function-60e44483-2ed1-439f-8bd0-e404c190949a (worked example, WEEKDAY(A2)=5)

WEEKDAY_type2_monday_start.description: return_type 2: Monday=1..Sunday=7. Source: Microsoft WEEKDAY docs worked example, WEEKDAY(A2,2)=4.

WEEKDAY_type3_zero_based.description: return_type 3: Monday=0..Sunday=6 (zero-based). Thursday is 3 in this scheme.
WEEKDAY_type3_zero_based.notes: Per Microsoft's return_type table, mode 3 maps Monday..Sunday to 0..6; Thursday (the 4th weekday counting Monday=0) = 3.

WEEKDAY_type12_tuesday_start.description: return_type 12: Tuesday=1..Monday=7 (ISO-style variant start day). Thursday is the 3rd day in this scheme.
WEEKDAY_type12_tuesday_start.notes: Per Microsoft's return_type table, mode 12 maps Tuesday=1,Wed=2,Thu=3,...,Monday=7.

WEEKDAY_type16_saturday_start.description: return_type 16: Saturday=1..Friday=7. Thursday is the 6th day in this scheme.
WEEKDAY_type16_saturday_start.notes: Per Microsoft's return_type table, mode 16 maps Saturday=1,Sun=2,Mon=3,Tue=4,Wed=5,Thu=6,Fri=7.

WEEKDAY_invalid_return_type.description: An out-of-range return_type (not one of the documented 1,2,3,11-17 values) is a documented error condition.
WEEKDAY_invalid_return_type.notes: Microsoft docs: an invalid return_type raises #NUM!.; MISMATCH vs expected: expected '#NUM!', got '#VALUE!'

WEEKNUM_us_default_return_type_1.description: return_type 1 = US system, weeks start Sunday, week containing Jan 1 is always week 1. Verified via https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/weeknum-function-e5c43a03-b4ab-426c-b411-b18c13c75340

WEEKNUM_monday_start_return_type_2.description: return_type 2 = same 'week containing Jan 1 is week 1' system, but weeks start Monday
WEEKNUM_monday_start_return_type_2.notes: Jan 1, 2024 happens to BE a Monday, so it is both the week-1-by-Sunday-rule week AND the very first day of the week-1-by-Monday-rule week -- return_type 1 and 2 coincide at 1 for this specific date (see WEEKNUM_doc_example_* below for a date where they genuinely diverge)

WEEKNUM_doc_example_default.description: Microsoft's own documented example date/result for the default return_type (1)
WEEKNUM_doc_example_default.notes: Per Microsoft docs, WEEKNUM(3/9/2012) = 10 with default return_type

WEEKNUM_doc_example_monday_start_diverge.description: Same date as WEEKNUM_doc_example_default but return_type=2 (Monday-start): Microsoft's own docs show this returns a DIFFERENT week number (11, not 10) for the identical date -- the genuine return_type divergence
WEEKNUM_doc_example_monday_start_diverge.notes: Per Microsoft docs, WEEKNUM(3/9/2012,2) = 11, one more than the return_type=1 result of 10

WEEKNUM_return_type_21_is_iso_system.description: return_type 21 uses 'System 2': the week containing the year's first Thursday is week 1 (ISO 8601), a genuinely different algorithm from return_types 1/2/11-17 (all of which use 'week containing Jan 1 = week 1'). This should match ISOWEEKNUM(DATE(2023,1,1)) exactly -- see ISOWEEKNUM.json
WEEKNUM_return_type_21_is_iso_system.notes: Jan 1, 2023 is a Sunday; under the ISO rule it falls in the LAST ISO week (52) of 2022, not week 1 of 2023

WORKDAY_basic_no_holidays.description: 5 working days after Mon Jan 1, 2024, no holidays supplied
WORKDAY_basic_no_holidays.notes: Jan 1, 2024 (Mon) + 5 workdays, skipping the Jan 6-7 weekend, = Jan 8, 2024 = serial 45299

WORKDAY_with_single_holiday.description: Same start/offset as WORKDAY_basic_no_holidays but with Jan 3 excluded as a holiday, pushing the result one calendar day later
WORKDAY_with_single_holiday.notes: With Jan 3 excluded, the 5th workday becomes Jan 9, 2024 = serial 45300

WORKDAY_multiple_holidays_via_range.description: holidays argument as a multi-cell range (Jan 3 and Jan 4 both excluded)
WORKDAY_multiple_holidays_via_range.notes: With both Jan 3 and Jan 4 excluded, the 5th workday becomes Jan 10, 2024 = serial 45301

WORKDAY_negative_days_goes_backward.description: Negative days argument counts backward from start_date instead of forward. Verified via https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/workday-function-f764a5b7-05fc-4494-9486-60d494efbf33 ("A positive value for days yields a future date; a negative value yields a past date")
WORKDAY_negative_days_goes_backward.notes: Jan 10, 2024 (Wed) - 5 workdays, skipping the Jan 6-7 weekend, = Jan 3, 2024 = serial 45294

WRAPCOLS_2.description: Wrap a 4-cell row into a grid 2 tall, filling by column
WRAPCOLS_2.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 1, got '#NAME?'

WRAPROWS_2.description: Wrap a 4-cell row into a 2-column grid, filling by row
WRAPROWS_2.notes: MISMATCH vs expected: value mismatch: expected 1, got '#NAME?'

XLOOKUP_exact_match.description: Basic exact-match lookup returns the corresponding value

XLOOKUP_not_found_default_na.description: No if_not_found arg and no match -> #N/A

XLOOKUP_not_found_custom.description: Custom if_not_found value is returned when no match

XLOOKUP_match_mode_next_smaller.description: match_mode -1 (exact or next smaller item) with no exact match
XLOOKUP_match_mode_next_smaller.notes: 2.5 has no exact match; next-smaller is 2 -> 20; MISMATCH vs expected: expected 20, got '#NAME?'

XLOOKUP_search_mode_last_to_first.description: search_mode -1 finds the LAST occurrence of a duplicated key
XLOOKUP_search_mode_last_to_first.notes: Two rows match 'x' (1 and 3); reverse search returns the later one; MISMATCH vs expected: expected 3, got '#NAME?'

XLOOKUP_empty_lookup_array.description: Degenerate 1-cell lookup/return arrays where the cell is blank
XLOOKUP_empty_lookup_array.notes: A1 and B1 are blank; lookup value 'a' is not found; MISMATCH vs expected: expected '#N/A', got '#NAME?'

XOR_odd_count_of_true.description: An ODD number of TRUE arguments (3) -> TRUE

XOR_even_count_of_true.description: An EVEN number of TRUE arguments (2) -> FALSE, matching docs' own example =XOR(3>0,2<9) -> FALSE

XOR_zero_true_is_even.description: Zero TRUE arguments counts as even -> FALSE, matching docs' own example =XOR(3>12,4>6) -> FALSE

XOR_single_true.description: A single TRUE argument (odd count = 1) -> TRUE

XOR_range_numeric_coercion.description: Numbers in a range are coerced to logical (0=FALSE, nonzero=TRUE); here two nonzero values -> 2 TRUEs (even) -> FALSE

YEAR_of_date.description: YEAR applied to a constructed date

YEAR_of_raw_serial_epoch.description: YEAR applied directly to the raw serial number 1 (the classic Excel epoch, serial 1 = Jan 1, 1900)
YEAR_of_raw_serial_epoch.notes: Serial 1 is Jan 1, 1900 in the 1900 date system; MISMATCH vs expected: expected 1900, got 1899

YEAR_ignores_time_fraction.description: A serial with a fractional (time-of-day) component still resolves to the correct calendar year
YEAR_ignores_time_fraction.notes: 45366 = 2024-03-15; the .75 fractional day (18:00) is ignored by YEAR

YEARFRAC_basis0_us_nasd_default.description: Default basis 0 (US/NASD 30/360): treats every month as 30 days. Days = 6*30+29 = 209, over a 360-day year -> 209/360. Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/yearfrac-function-3844141e-c76d-4143-82b6-208454ddc6a8 (worked example rounds to 0.58055556)

YEARFRAC_basis1_actual_actual.description: basis 1 (Actual/actual): actual 211 calendar days between the two dates, divided by 366 since the enclosing 1-year span (Jan 2012-Jan 2013) is a leap year. Source: Microsoft YEARFRAC docs worked example rounds to 0.57650273.
YEARFRAC_basis1_actual_actual.notes: 211/366 = 0.5765027322404372, matching Microsoft's documented rounded example (0.57650273) to 8 decimal places. Actual/actual leap-year-boundary handling is a documented high-divergence area across spreadsheet engines.

YEARFRAC_basis2_actual_360.description: basis 2 (Actual/360): actual 211 calendar days divided by a fixed 360-day year. Source: Microsoft YEARFRAC docs basis table.

YEARFRAC_basis3_actual_365.description: basis 3 (Actual/365): actual 211 calendar days divided by a fixed 365-day year. Source: Microsoft YEARFRAC docs worked example rounds to 0.57808219.
YEARFRAC_basis3_actual_365.notes: 211/365 = 0.5780821917808219, matches Microsoft's documented rounded example exactly.

YEARFRAC_basis4_european_30_360.description: basis 4 (European 30/360). For this particular date pair neither day-of-month is the 31st, so the European day-count adjustment never triggers and the result equals basis 0's 209/360.
YEARFRAC_basis4_european_30_360.notes: European 30/360 only differs from US 30/360 when a start/end day-of-month is 31; with day 1 and day 30 as here, both conventions compute 209/360 identically.

YEARFRAC_invalid_basis_error.description: basis values outside 0-4 are documented as invalid.
YEARFRAC_invalid_basis_error.notes: Microsoft docs: 'invalid basis values (< 0 or > 4) return a #NUM! error.'; MISMATCH vs expected: expected '#NUM!', got '#VALUE!'
